label: delphi-r1-p6
system:
You are performing one specific cognitive operation as an isolated component of a legal-exploration pipeline. You never see the other components' outputs, and nobody sees yours until a separate neutral aggregation step. Ground rules for every component:
- The scenario is an invented hypothetical; there is no real client. Your output goes to a licensed California attorney who will do the actual legal reasoning. You generate angles to pursue and things to verify — not advice to be relied on.
- Cite specific authorities (statutes, cases, regulations) when they genuinely support a point. Every citation is automatically flagged UNVERIFIED for human checking, so cite what you believe is correct, but never fabricate quotations or pinpoint holdings you are unsure of. When unsure, write "verify whether X holds Y" instead of asserting it.
- Perform ONLY the operation described below. Do not produce a general-purpose legal memo.

Operation: you are ONE independent panelist in a Delphi elicitation. There is no
discussion; you answer alone. Give your honest probability, not a diplomatic one.
Do not hedge toward 50 to be safe — commit to your analysis.
prompt:
## Facts (invented hypothetical — no real parties)

The City of Piedra Blanca (invented) is renegotiating its 15-year exclusive solid-waste franchise, worth roughly $180M over its term. A consultant for one bidder, GreenCycle Environmental, created a Signal group called "PB Zero Waste Future" and added three of the five councilmembers, several GreenCycle executives, and two community advocates. Disappearing messages were set to one week. Over three months, participants discussed franchise terms, diversion targets, "what council will realistically accept," and the timing of the RFP release. The members participated from personal phones; nothing was ever forwarded to city accounts. One councilmember, uneasy, screenshotted about 40 exchanges before they auto-deleted; the rest are gone.

After losing the eventual award to GreenCycle, competing bidder Pacific Refuse submitted a CPRA request for "all communications by any councilmember concerning the franchise, on any platform or account." The city clerk searched city systems and responded "no responsive records." Pacific Refuse has now sued for declaratory and injunctive relief under the CPRA, and separately demanded the city preserve and produce the surviving screenshots. The city's retention schedule, adopted under Government Code § 34090, requires retention of correspondence relating to franchises for four years.

## The question

Are the Signal messages "public records" under City of San Jose v. Superior Court (2017) 2 Cal.5th 608 even though they sat on personal devices and most no longer exist? What search and inquiry did the CPRA obligate the city to conduct before answering "no responsive records," and did the clerk's answer violate the Act? Does the auto-deletion violate § 34090 or any retention duty as applied to records on personal accounts, and what remedies exist for records that are gone (the CPRA has no damages provision) — declaratory relief, fees, adverse findings in the franchise litigation, referral? Do the three members' communications in a shared group about franchise terms create Brown Act exposure that interacts with the CPRA claim? What should the city do now, and what policy should it adopt going forward?

## Proposition to assess

"A court would hold that the councilmembers' Signal messages concerning the franchise were public records under the CPRA, and that the city's "no responsive records" response, made without any inquiry to the members about personal-account communications, violated the Act."

Estimate the probability (0–100) that this proposition is correct, with your reasoning. rationales = your independent load-bearing reasons, one sentence each. authorities = specific authorities you are actually relying on (they will be flagged for human verification). biggest_uncertainty = the single thing you are least sure of.

Analytical starting point for you: Begin from close analogies: how have adjacent or analogous situations been treated?
text: {"probability":78,"confidence":"medium","position_summary":"More likely than not (and fairly strongly so) that a court finds both halves of the conjunctive proposition true: the Signal messages were \"public records\" under the City of San Jose framework, and the clerk's city-systems-only search, never reaching out to the three councilmembers despite a request explicitly covering \"any platform or account,\" fell short of the Act's reasonable-search obligation. The strongest single data point is the undisclosed 40 surviving screenshots — a concrete, known-to-exist responsive record that a proper inquiry would have surfaced.","rationales":["City of San Jose v. Superior Court (2017) 2 Cal.5th 608 holds a writing relating to conduct of public business, retained by an individual official on a personal account, satisfies Gov. Code § 6252(e); messages about franchise terms, diversion targets, 'what council will realistically accept,' and RFP timing are arguably a cleaner fit for that test than San Jose's own mixed personal/official texts.","A majority bloc (three of five councilmembers) discussing deal terms on a pending $180M award goes to the heart of 'conduct of the public's business,' strengthening both the substantive-nexus and the 'retained by' prongs San Jose construed broadly to reach individual custodians acting in their official capacity.","Gov. Code § 6252.5 (added by AB 700, eff. 2022) specifically requires agencies to contact officials and can require a signed declaration when a request could reasonably encompass personal-account communications by elected officials, and Pacific Refuse's request expressly demanded records 'on any platform or account,' making a city-systems-only search facially non-compliant with both that statute and the general 'reasonably calculated' search standard.","The one councilmember's 40 surviving screenshots were known (at least to that member) to exist at the time of the response, so a search that never asked any councilmember anything is close to a per se inadequate search independent of how the broader legal test is resolved.","Countervailing factor: San Jose expressly left open how agencies should structure searches of personal accounts and did not address records already destroyed before a request arrives, so a court could still find the messages were public records while treating the 'no responsive records' answer as a closer, more fact-bound question rather than an automatic statutory violation — this tempers full confidence in the conjunctive proposition as stated."],"authorities":["City of San Jose v. Superior Court (2017) 2 Cal.5th 608","Gov. Code § 6252(e) (public records definition; note 2023 CPRA recodification renumbered many sections into the 7920.000–7931.000 series — verify current citation)","Gov. Code § 6252.5, added by AB 700 (Stats. 2021, ch. 456) — personal-account search/declaration duty for elected officials (verify current recodified section number)","American Civil Liberties Union of Northern California v. Superior Court (2011) 202 Cal.App.4th 55 — 'reasonably calculated' search standard","Gov. Code § 34090 — local agency records retention/destruction authority, relevant to the separate deletion/retention-duty question"],"biggest_uncertainty":"Whether a court will treat the 'no responsive records' answer as a clean, per se Act violation (given the explicit request language and the known screenshots) or instead as a fact-bound adequacy-of-search question on which the city could still partially prevail by showing some good-faith limitation, especially since San Jose and its progeny have not squarely resolved search-methodology standards for already-largely-destroyed ephemeral personal-app messages."}

data.position_summary: More likely than not (and fairly strongly so) that a court finds both halves of the conjunctive proposition true: the Signal messages were "public records" under the City of San Jose framework, and the clerk's city-systems-only search, never reaching out to the three councilmembers despite a request explicitly covering "any platform or account," fell short of the Act's reasonable-search obligation. The strongest single data point is the undisclosed 40 surviving screenshots — a concrete, known-to-exist responsive record that a proper inquiry would have surfaced.
rationales: City of San Jose v. Superior Court (2017) 2 Cal.5th 608 holds a writing relating to conduct of public business, retained by an individual official on a personal account, satisfies Gov. Code § 6252(e); messages about franchise terms, diversion targets, 'what council will realistically accept,' and RFP timing are arguably a cleaner fit for that test than San Jose's own mixed personal/official texts.

A majority bloc (three of five councilmembers) discussing deal terms on a pending $180M award goes to the heart of 'conduct of the public's business,' strengthening both the substantive-nexus and the 'retained by' prongs San Jose construed broadly to reach individual custodians acting in their official capacity.

Gov. Code § 6252.5 (added by AB 700, eff. 2022) specifically requires agencies to contact officials and can require a signed declaration when a request could reasonably encompass personal-account communications by elected officials, and Pacific Refuse's request expressly demanded records 'on any platform or account,' making a city-systems-only search facially non-compliant with both that statute and the general 'reasonably calculated' search standard.

The one councilmember's 40 surviving screenshots were known (at least to that member) to exist at the time of the response, so a search that never asked any councilmember anything is close to a per se inadequate search independent of how the broader legal test is resolved.

Countervailing factor: San Jose expressly left open how agencies should structure searches of personal accounts and did not address records already destroyed before a request arrives, so a court could still find the messages were public records while treating the 'no responsive records' answer as a closer, more fact-bound question rather than an automatic statutory violation — this tempers full confidence in the conjunctive proposition as stated.
authorities: City of San Jose v. Superior Court (2017) 2 Cal.5th 608
Gov. Code § 6252(e) (public records definition; note 2023 CPRA recodification renumbered many sections into the 7920.000–7931.000 series — verify current citation)
Gov. Code § 6252.5, added by AB 700 (Stats. 2021, ch. 456) — personal-account search/declaration duty for elected officials (verify current recodified section number)
American Civil Liberties Union of Northern California v. Superior Court (2011) 202 Cal.App.4th 55 — 'reasonably calculated' search standard
Gov. Code § 34090 — local agency records retention/destruction authority, relevant to the separate deletion/retention-duty question
data.biggest_uncertainty: Whether a court will treat the 'no responsive records' answer as a clean, per se Act violation (given the explicit request language and the known screenshots) or instead as a fact-bound adequacy-of-search question on which the city could still partially prevail by showing some good-faith limitation, especially since San Jose and its progeny have not squarely resolved search-methodology standards for already-largely-destroyed ephemeral personal-app messages.
